image.alt: Stethoscope resting on medical case notes beside a cup of tea

situation.headline: The situation
paragraphs: A medical practice with 12 physicians operated a defined-contribution retirement plan with high administrative costs and limited investment options. The plan had not been reviewed in six years.

Several physicians were within 10 years of retirement. Their benefit structures did not reflect their equity stakes in the practice, and contribution limits were not optimised for their income levels.

approach.headline: Our approach
paragraphs: We audited the existing plan documents, fee disclosures, and fund lineup. We then benchmarked the plan against comparable medical practice plans in the same revenue band.

We redesigned the plan with a new recordkeeper, lower-cost index fund options, and a profit-sharing formula aligned to ownership percentages. We also added a cash-balance component for the physicians closest to retirement, giving them a higher annual contribution ceiling.

outcome.headline: The outcome
paragraphs: Administrative overhead dropped by 40%. The average all-in fund expense ratio fell from 0.82% to 0.11%. Senior physicians now contribute up to three times more per year through the cash-balance layer.

The practice runs quarterly governance reviews on a fixed schedule. Each physician receives an individual projection that maps their plan balance to a retirement income estimate. The plan is now in its second year under the new structure.
